title: figs. An important part of this is the understanding of the ecology of Aspergillus species in connection with dried figs.

sections.section: figs. An important part of this is the understanding of the ecology of Aspergillus species in connection with dried figs.
sections.summary: Emphasizes that understanding the ecology of Aspergillus species in relation to dried figs is an important component of managing contamination risk.

sections.section: 4. Mycotoxins,  in  particular  aflatoxins  are  secondary  metabolites  produced  by  filamentous fungus found in soil, air and all plant parts and can be toxic to human and animals through consumption of contaminated food and feed entering into food chain.
sections.summary: States that mycotoxins—particularly aflatoxins—are secondary metabolites from filamentous fungi found in soil, air and plant parts that can be toxic to humans and animals, notes aflatoxin B1 is genotoxic/carcinogenic, and that aflatoxins are produced in warm, humid conditions and occur mainly in commodities from tropical/subtropical regions (e.g. peanuts, other nuts, dried fruit, spices, maize) and can contaminate milk/milk products as aflatoxin M1 via contaminated animal feed.

sections.section: 5. Aflatoxigenic  fungi  are  spread  on  fig  fruits  during  fruit  growth,  ripening  and  drying  but thrive especially during the ripening and overripening phase. The formation of aflatoxins in dried figs is mainly due to contamination by Aspergillus species and particularly A. flavus and A. parasiticus . The presence and spread of such fungus in fig orchards are influenced by environmental and climatic factors, insects, (insect abundance or control in an orchard is related to the applied plant protection measures so could be included in cultural practices but  to  point  out  its  significance  can  be  left  as  another  factor),  cultural  practices,  floor management and susceptibility of fig varieties.
sections.summary: Explains that aflatoxigenic fungi—especially Aspergillus flavus and A. parasiticus—spread to fig fruits during growth, ripening and drying (peaking in ripening/overripening), and that environmental/climatic factors, insects, cultural practices, floor management and fig variety susceptibility influence their presence and spread.

sections.section: 6. The aflatoxin-producing Aspergillus species and consequently dietary aflatoxin, contamination is  ubiquitous  in  areas  of  the  world  with  hot  humid  climates. A.  flavus / A. parasiticus cannot  grow  or  produce  aflatoxins  at  water  activities  less  than  0.7;  relative humidity  below  70%  and  temperatures  below  10  ºC.  Under  stress  conditions  such  as drought or insect infestation, aflatoxin contamination is likely to be high. Improper storage conditions  can  also  lead  to  aflatoxin  contamination  after  crops  have  been  harvested. Usually, hot humid conditions favour mould growth on the stored food which can lead to high levels of aflatoxins.
sections.summary: Notes that aflatoxin-producing Aspergillus are ubiquitous in hot, humid regions and cannot grow or produce aflatoxins below water activity 0.7, relative humidity under 70% or temperatures below 10°C, while drought, insect infestation or improper storage can increase aflatoxin contamination.

sections.section: 7. Application of the following preventive measures is recommended in dried fig producing regions in order to reduce aflatoxin contamination by application of good practices: a) Information on contamination risk.
sections.summary: Recommends preventive measures for dried-fig producing regions: authorities and grower organisations should sample dried-fig lots representatively across areas, seasons and production stages, combine results with regional risk factors and meteorological data, and communicate findings (including storage labelling) to operators and consumers; producers, transporters and storage operators should be trained in mould/mycotoxin risks and preventive measures (with documentation), contaminated lots managed appropriately, and related research encouraged. It also emphasizes that planting, pre‑/post‑harvest and processing strategies must be adapted to local climatic conditions and production practices.

Provides guidance for all parties producing and handling dried figs for international trade, requiring compliance with general food hygiene and dried-fruits codes, explains figs' heightened aflatoxin risk due to juicy pulpy skin and internal cavity allowing fungal growth (critical period from ripening through over‑ripening and drying) and notes insect vectors (e.g. Carpophilus and Drosophila), and states the main requirement is to obtain healthy plants and good-quality product via appropriate agricultural techniques.

Advises orchard management under Good Agricultural Practice—timely caprification, pruning, tillage, fertilization, irrigation and plant protection—to minimize Aspergillus dispersion, cease soil tillage one month before harvest, maintain roadways, avoid equipment damage, remove and not directly incorporate pruned material (compost after analysis), base fertilization on soil/plant analysis (avoid excess nitrogen), apply integrated pest management with records, irrigate to reduce tree stress while preventing water contact with figs, and use irrigation/water of suitable quality per legislation.

Describes caprification controls: use healthy, fungus‑free caprifigs with live pollen and wasps, remove rotten/soft caprifigs before use, generally avoid male trees in female orchards, and collect and destroy caprifigs after caprification (recommending placement in nets or bags to facilitate removal) to limit fungal and pest transfer.

Requires harvesting equipment and machinery to be inspected, clean and not hazardous, directs trade associations and authorities to develop guidance informing growers about aflatoxin hazards and safe harvesting, and mandates training of harvesting personnel in personal hygiene and sanitary practices.

Specifies dried-fig harvesting practices: overripe figs that fall should be collected daily in suitable, clean, non‑damaging containers to reduce aflatoxin formation, disease and pest losses; harvesting should occur at short intervals and dew formation from large day–night temperature differences should be managed as it can promote fungal growth.

Sets drying requirements: control drying area and time (partially dried figs ≈30–50% moisture; fully dried ≈20–22%), prefer artificial driers but, if sun‑drying, use elevated (10–15 cm), covered trays and avoid placing fruit on soil to reduce contamination, collect figs from trays when moisture ≤24% and water activity ≈0.65 (preferably in the morning after dew), apply permitted pest treatments, and dry/store low‑quality culls separately with trained staff.

Requires transport measures that protect dried‑fig quality: prevent moisture/temperature increases and cross‑contamination with odorous products, use clean, dry, aerated containers (not bags) and clean vehicles, seal containers to exclude dust/insects, and empty and clean transport containers at unloading.

Specifies storage controls: store cleaned, dried and labelled figs in facilities with temperature and moisture control (aim for water activity <0.65; recommended 5–10°C and relative humidity <65%) to prevent mould and secondary aflatoxin formation, prevent direct contact with floors/walls, eliminate humidity sources, separate low‑quality figs, disinfect storage rooms and protect against pests and odour contamination.

Covers processing controls: incoming lots must be sampled for moisture and screened for bright greenish yellow fluorescence (BGYF) under long‑wave UV (360 nm) to identify and remove potentially aflatoxin‑contaminated figs (which must be labelled and destroyed), maintain moisture ≤24% and water activity <0.65 (and re‑dry if washing raises moisture), use potable water for washing, apply permitted pest treatments, ensure hygienic equipment and facilities, and treat processing measures as corrective while prevention is primarily achieved in orchards.
